payload.body: CodeGraph is ready for cross-chat testing. Implemented launch-anywhere bootstrap with codegraph init [path], self-bootstrapping codegraph mcp [path], Claude hooks, AGENTS.md for Codex guidance, MCP graph auto-refresh via SQLite data_version, incremental index skipping unchanged files, repository doctor checks, bench index, and test-acceptance covering design_spec section 13. Important caveat: durable memory is currently written to .codegraph/ops, but .codegraph/ is gitignored, so this memory will not survive push/clone unless op logs/config are explicitly tracked or sync/handoff is implemented.
payload.title: Step 11 acceptance-ready state

payload.body: Smoke test handoff written through the rebuilt CodeGraph MCP server. Context: validating the new write_handoff tool after rebuild/restart.
payload.title: Codex handoff smoke test

payload.body: Reviewed HANDOFF.md and current working tree on 2026-06-22. HANDOFF.md documents completed milestones through Step 11: doctor, bench index, and acceptance tests, with next milestone listed as post-acceptance polish. Current uncommitted work extends that baseline: src/cpp_frontend.cpp now recurses through anonymous namespace/class-like scopes without emitting a scope symbol, and src/main.cpp adds a test fixture proving functions inside anonymous namespaces are indexed. Handoff MCP work is in progress: src/memory_reads.* now models ResumeContext with found, affected_nodes, unresolved_affects, and hydrated AffectedNodeView data; src/mcp_server.cpp returns the actual affected node JSON from resume_from_handoff instead of an empty vector. src/mcp_server.h no longer owns the old reduced AffectedNodeView. MCP launch/debug work is also present: .mcp.json now uses VS Code style servers config launching /bin/bash with codegraph-mcp.sh, and codegraph-mcp.sh logs wrapper context before execing build-ninja/codegraph mcp /home/yash/codegraph. Verification performed after review: cmake --build build-ninja reported no work to do; ./build-ninja/codegraph test-mcp passed; ./build-ninja/codegraph test-memory passed; ./build-ninja/codegraph test-index passed when rerun sequentially; ./build-ninja/codegraph test-acceptance passed when rerun sequentially. A parallel test attempt caused transient shared-test-db failures, not reproduced sequentially. Known issue in live repo DB: ./build-ninja/codegraph doctor /home/yash/codegraph currently fails with nodes_bad_kind: 1 because the live graph contains a handoff node; the isolated acceptance doctor still passes. Next agent should update doctor vocabulary/checks to accept handoff nodes, consider adding resume_from_handoff coverage to test-mcp, and decide whether codegraph-mcp.sh plus .mcp.json should be committed or treated as local VS Code troubleshooting.
payload.title: Post-Step 11 working-tree handoff

payload.body:
Step 12 complete: added list_symbols_in_file MCP tool. Traversal lives in the graph layer as graph_symbols_in_file(index, file_node) in src/graph_store.cpp (recursive forward-Contains walk returning {symbol_node, parent_node} pairs, peer to graph_symbols_by_name_hash); src/mcp_server.cpp only serializes (symbol_json + parent_node_id, sorted by start_line), registered in tool_registry() and read_tool_name(). test-mcp id 11 asserts recursion (nested mcpstep::target present) and parent tracking (parent_node_id == namespace node, not file). All suites pass: test-mcp, test-graph, test-acceptance. Caveat: anonymous-namespace/file-local symbols are not yet extracted so they are absent from listings (upstream cpp_frontend.cpp gap, in-tree fix targets it). enclosing_symbol left inline in mcp_server.cpp on purpose (now the only Contains-traversal not in graph_store; future cleanup).

v2 thesis (from dogfooding): tool is "nice to have"; becomes "need to have" only when (1) trust/completeness holds so empty means absent not missed — agents stop falling back to rg, (2) discovery works by intent not just exact names (search_symbol currently fails intent queries; indexes names+signatures with whole-token matching), (3) accumulated memory (corrections/decisions at edit time) is the irreplaceable moat. Trust is precondition, intent-search is entry point, memory is moat.

v2 sequence: A) Trust — land anon-namespace extraction fix, add coverage honesty (report unindexed paths), fix doctor handoff-node vocabulary. B) Benchmark harness (tool-vs-no-tool, tokens + success) BEFORE the reference graph — it is the instrument that decides whether/where Tier 2 earns its cost and the corpus that surfaces wrong/missing references. C) Source-text FTS search + find_prior_incidents together (shared fts_*+trigger infra). D) Reference/call graph, tiered and gated on the benchmark: Tier 1 tree-sitter syntactic Calls/References edges with a resolution field (syntactic|resolved) baked into the schema from day one; then clangd Tier 2 for C++ upgrading edges to resolved where compile_commands.json exists, degrading gracefully to syntactic when clangd/compile DB absent. Validate Tier 2 on this repo (CMAKE_EXPORT_COMPILE_COMMANDS=ON). Decoupled from Python — clangd does not wait for Python; Pyright rides with the Python frontend later. Defer new language frontends. Full detail in HANDOFF.md Step 12 + Next milestone: v2.
payload.title: Step 12 done (list_symbols_in_file) + v2 plan

payload.body:
First v2 milestone-A (trust) task complete: memory writes now report which affects targets did not resolve, instead of leaving the caller to infer it from a counter.

Context/motivation: a write_handoff returned edges_resolved:0 which looked like failure. Investigation (querying .codegraph/graph.sqlite for node 2240) showed 4 of 5 affects edges actually resolved inline at materialize time; only HANDOFF.md stayed pending because markdown is not an indexed file type (resolve_file matches nodes.title against indexed file nodes, resolver.cpp:14). edges_resolved only counts edges newly resolved by the deferred resolver pass, so a fully-successful write can show 0. The real gap was reporting/coverage-honesty, not reindex timing. (Confirmed: reindex-before-read would break verify-before-trust + data_version freshness and the O(symbol) read latency; reindex-after-write is unnecessary since a memory write changes no source. Source reindex already runs incrementally via Step 10 hooks on edits.)

Change:
- src/mcp_server.cpp: added unresolved_affects_for_node(storage, node_id) selecting affects edges with resolved=0 for the new memory node; write_memory now returns an unresolved_affects array alongside op_id/node_id/ops_applied/edges_resolved. record_correction, record_decision, and write_handoff all funnel through write_memory, so all three report it.
- src/main.cpp (test-mcp): id 8 record_correction on an indexed file must report empty unresolved_affects; new id 12 record_decision affecting docs/UNTRACKED.md (not indexed) must report ["docs/UNTRACKED.md"]. Response count now 12.

Verified: test-mcp and full test-acceptance pass. Note the live codegraph mcp server is the pre-change binary, so the new field appears after that server restarts.

Still open in A (not started): fix doctor handoff-node vocabulary (nodes_bad_kind failure); land in-tree anon-namespace extraction fix + coverage honesty for unindexed paths; diagnose duplicate file nodes (e.g. src/graph_store.cpp exists as nodes 834 and 2008; src/main.cpp as 68 and 2024) — likely a bootstrap/reindex bug worth fixing at source before adding a doctor check. Remaining v2 order after A: B benchmark harness, C source-text FTS + find_prior_incidents, D tiered reference/call graph. See HANDOFF.md.
payload.title: v2-A: unresolved-affects reporting on memory writes

payload.body: Source graph cache validity is keyed by an explicit kSourceProjectionVersion persisted per file; extractor or persisted projection changes must bump that version so unchanged source is re-extracted. If repo_id changes, foreign source nodes are removed, affected-memory edges are reset and re-resolved, and files are marked for projection rebuild to prevent duplicate or missing graph nodes. MCP reliability/cost is measured through benchmarks/mcp_benchmark.py against a disposable fixture, with zero tolerated tool errors and raw latency/byte metrics plus a labeled character-based token estimate.
payload.title: Source projection freshness and MCP benchmark contract

payload.body: CodeGraph is the verification/durable-memory layer, not the primary repository search layer. AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md permit normal agentic discovery and require CodeGraph verification/memory reads before acting on relevant code. Codex and Claude hooks automatically inject verified resume context on SessionStart, refresh after mutation-capable tools, block manual compaction while dirty, and continue the agent at Stop to write a semantic handoff. Hook scripts do not synthesize handoff prose themselves; the active agent calls write_handoff. The product benchmark is a controlled multi-session task experiment comparing flat HANDOFF.md with CodeGraph under no-drift and drift conditions; the existing MCP benchmark is only a protocol smoke test.
payload.title: Agent lifecycle implements verified resume and semantic handoff

payload.body: The purpose-built request-scheduler experiment fixture lives at /home/yash/request_scheduler. Preserve the package split: fixture/ is the only agent-visible Git worktree; control/ remains controller-only and contains hidden tests, drift patches, drift_manifest.json, gold patches, matched AGENTS templates, prompts, and verification scripts. The base has 43 C++ files and one visible priority-order failure. Session 1 fixes descending priority, Drift 1 moves/renames build_flush_plan to PlanComposer::compose, Session 2 fixes max_batch_size in policy::selectable_count, Drift 2 deletes append_if_eligible and config_rules and folds them into EligibilityGate, and Session 3 implements background deferral. Do not put hidden tests or drift refs inside the agent-visible repository, because that leaks ground truth and weakens the experiment.
payload.title: Verified-resume fixture isolates agent worktree from controller ground truth

payload.body:
Objective achieved: built the synthetic multi-session C++ fixture specified by docs/verified_resume_experiment.md under /home/yash/request_scheduler.

Completed:
- Package split: fixture/ is a clean single-branch Git repo at base commit 3d1cda6; control/ is private ground truth and must not be agent-visible.
- Base fixture: 43 C++ source/header/test files, deterministic, no external dependencies, one visible priority-order failure, named invariant documented.
- Task chain: S1 descending priority repair; Drift 1 moves/renames build_flush_plan to PlanComposer::compose; S2 max_batch_size edge case in policy::selectable_count; Drift 2 deletes append_if_eligible/config_rules and folds behavior into EligibilityGate; S3 background deferral.
- Added exhaustive drift_manifest.json, matched baseline/treatment AGENTS templates, exact prompts, hidden tests, gold patches, ground_truth.md, and prepare/apply/verify scripts.
- Drift patches apply automatically with git am --3way over expected session edits; an earlier conflict was removed by separating the session-2 batch-policy seam from Drift 2's planner/eligibility seam.

Verification:
- /home/yash/request_scheduler/control/verify_chain.sh passes end-to-end.
- Final gold state: 7/7 visible and 5/5 hidden tests pass.
- Immediately before S3: 6/6 visible pass and only background_deferral_hidden_test fails.
- Installed fixture repo is clean and exposes only main/origin-main; hidden tests and drift refs are outside it.

Next action: build the experiment harness from section 9 around control/prepare_run.sh and control/apply_drift.sh, including JSONL trace capture and matched handoff payload normalization. No fixture blocker remains.
payload.title: Request-scheduler verified-resume fixture implemented and verified

payload.body:
Current objective: set up and execute the controlled baseline-vs-CodeGraph verified-resume experiment. Success means isolated baseline/treatment worktrees, three fresh Codex sessions per arm, controller-applied drift between sessions, hidden ground truth inaccessible to agents, and trace data sufficient for deterministic scoring.

Completed:
- Built and verified the private request-scheduler fixture at /home/yash/request_scheduler.
- fixture/ is the agent-visible Git repo at base commit 3d1cda6 with 43 deterministic C++ files and one intentional visible priority-order failure.
- control/ contains hidden tests, drift-1/drift-2 patches, exhaustive drift_manifest.json, exact session prompts, matched AGENTS templates, gold patches, and setup/verification scripts.
- control/verify_chain.sh passes the full expected chain: 7/7 visible and 5/5 hidden tests pass at completion; before session 3 only hidden background deferral fails.
- Confirmed /home/yash/codegraph/baseline_agent.md is the intended baseline AGENTS.md and is matched clause-for-clause in substance against the treatment rules.
- Clarified that GitHub is optional; use two isolated local clones per run. Each arm uses the same worktree across its three sessions, but every session is a fresh Codex chat. control/ must not be inside either agent workspace.

Exact next action:
1. Create pilot worktrees with /home/yash/request_scheduler/control/prepare_run.sh baseline|treatment.
2. Initialize/configure CodeGraph only in the treatment worktree.
3. Run session 1 in separate fresh Codex chats using the exact prompt in control/session_prompts.txt.
4. Capture and normalize the session-1 handoff payload/anchors so both arms receive identical content.
5. Apply drift-1 externally with control/apply_drift.sh, then run fresh session 2; repeat for drift-2/session 3.
6. Add/finish the harness trace logger before treating results as experimental evidence; manual chats alone cannot compute stale-action and token/tool metrics.

Blockers: no fixture blocker. The remaining engineering work is the section-9 harness, especially raw transcript/event capture and matched-payload normalization.

Unresolved questions: which Codex invocation/API will drive fresh sessions and expose token/tool event streams; whether to run a small manual pilot before automating the full 4-cell, >=10-run matrix.
payload.title: Verified-resume experiment ready for pilot run setup

payload.body:
Current objective: prepare and run the verified-resume baseline-vs-CodeGraph experiment correctly. Success requires clean, isolated run repositories; three fresh Codex sessions per chain; persistent but stale baseline HANDOFF.md across drift; persistent CodeGraph memory with live anchor re-resolution; identical normalized handoff payloads; hidden controller isolation; and complete traces for scoring.

Completed:
- Inspected /home/yash/request_scheduler_b, /home/yash/request_scheduler_cd, and shared controller /home/yash/codegraph/baseline_control/control.
- Confirmed baseline AGENTS.md correctly requires HANDOFF.md and that fixture .gitignore ignores /HANDOFF.md. Therefore the baseline handoff persists when the controller commits source changes and applies drift with git am.
- Confirmed treatment lifecycle: session-start resume-context bootstraps/refreshes the graph before resolving stored anchors, so externally applied drift is handled on the next fresh session. Stop hooks require write_handoff after mutations.
- Produced a detailed three-session walkthrough for both arms, including commit-before-drift, exact prompts, fresh-chat requirement, hidden scoring, failure handling, and the four-cell/120-chat full matrix.

Critical preflight findings:
1. Both current run folders wrap the actual Git repo inside fixture/. For a clean experiment, /home/yash/request_scheduler_b and /home/yash/request_scheduler_cd themselves should be the Git repository/workspace roots with AGENTS.md at that root.
2. The treatment fixture contains many Windows :Zone.Identifier files, including broken Git refs. It must be recreated from a clean clone; do not try to use it for a run.
3. The top-level README in each current run folder mentions control/ even though control is absent; recreate clean roots so agents receive only fixture documentation.
4. CodeGraph runtime/hook/MCP files must be installed only in treatment and excluded from Git. Baseline must have none.
5. Controller isolation must be enforced by filesystem/container sandboxing; merely keeping control outside Git is insufficient if arbitrary absolute paths remain readable.

Exact next action:
- Recreate both run directories from /home/yash/request_scheduler/fixture at commit 3d1cda6, putting fixture contents at each workspace root.
- Install baseline/treatment AGENTS.md as untracked operational files.
- Install and verify treatment CodeGraph binary link, hooks, .codex/hooks.json, MCP config/wrapper, and initialize .codegraph.
- Verify clean status, no Zone.Identifier files, no controller assets, baseline has no CodeGraph, and treatment session-start/find_symbol/write_handoff work.
- Then run one unscored six-session pilot before building/running the measured harness.

Blocker for a valid measured run: matched-payload normalization and event/token trace logging are not yet automated. Independently generated handoffs create a payload confound. Manual six-chat operation is acceptable only as a pilot.

Unresolved questions: which Codex invocation/API will provide raw tool/token event streams and enforce filesystem isolation; whether canonical pre-registered handoffs or captured-and-normalized payload installation will be used between paired arms.
payload.title: Experiment walkthrough defined; run workspaces require clean rebuild

payload.body:
Objective: verify /home/yash/request_scheduler_b and /home/yash/request_scheduler_cd before running the verified-resume pilot. Success requires clean fixture Git roots at 3d1cda6, no leaked controller assets, no CodeGraph in baseline, and a functional treatment-scoped CodeGraph MCP plus trusted lifecycle hooks.

Audit results:
- FAIL root layout: neither /home/yash/request_scheduler_b nor /home/yash/request_scheduler_cd is a Git repo; each wraps the repo under fixture/. Codex project-root discovery and hook git-root commands therefore do not match the intended workspace.
- Nested repos both point to exact commit 3d1cda6e7384c4a7a54abded978742696d8abea7. Their tracked archives are byte-identical (sha256 dd12844ea79efe150fad3868035e3be589248e346eded0d03739aff1defcb83a).
- Baseline nested repo is clean and contains zero Zone.Identifier files.
- Treatment nested repo is dirty with 259 :Zone.Identifier files, including Git object/ref sidecars; git fsck emits many bad-sha1 warnings. Reclone; do not repair in place.
- PASS controller file absence: neither workspace contains control/, hidden tests, drift patches, manifest, or gold files. However both top-level README.md files explicitly mention control/hidden/gold; recreating with fixture contents at root removes this metadata leak.
- AGENTS files exactly match controller templates by SHA-256.
- FAIL baseline isolation: project files contain no CodeGraph infrastructure, but active CODEX_HOME=/mnt/c/Users/yashm/.codex globally enables MCP servers named codegraph (targeting /home/yash/codegraph) and dual-graph. `codex mcp list` from the baseline shows both enabled. A baseline chat would therefore have graph tooling unless project overrides or a clean experiment CODEX_HOME disables them.
- FAIL treatment setup: no build/codegraph, hooks/codegraph_session.py, .codex/hooks.json, .codex/config.toml, .codegraph, or treatment-targeted MCP config exists at either treatment root or nested fixture root. Session-start resume, mutation refresh, and stop handoff enforcement cannot work.
- Official current Codex behavior verified: repo-specific MCP belongs in trusted project .codex/config.toml; hooks load from trusted .codex/hooks.json/config.toml and require review/trust. A legacy .mcp.json alone is not the documented Codex project configuration path.
- Fixture behavior is correct in both copies: builds succeed and exactly priority_order_test fails (4/5 visible tests pass), as expected at base.

Exact next action:
1. Move current run directories aside and clean-clone /home/yash/request_scheduler/fixture directly into each target path so .git is at workspace root.
2. Copy the matched AGENTS templates to each root and exclude operational files via .git/info/exclude.
3. Add baseline .codex/config.toml overrides disabling global codegraph, codegraph-local, and dual-graph (or use a clean experiment-specific CODEX_HOME).
4. Add treatment .codex/config.toml defining CodeGraph against /home/yash/request_scheduler_cd, disabling the globally mis-targeted graph servers; install build/codegraph, hooks/codegraph_session.py, and .codex/hooks.json; initialize .codegraph.
5. Trust both projects and explicitly review/trust treatment hooks. Verify with `codex mcp list`, `/mcp`, `/hooks`, resume-context, find_symbol, a controlled mutation/index refresh, and write_handoff/stop behavior.
6. Re-run the preflight before the six-chat pilot.

No run-directory files were modified during this audit. Blocker: do not start the pilot until the above failures are fixed.
payload.title: Preflight audit failed; recreate and isolate experiment workspaces
